text: Well, firstly, congratulations to Chemnitz, all the specialties, as this is the one hundredth Versius case done in Chemnitz. And for this one hundredth case, Sven Seifert and his team are performing a thymectomy with a right-sided approach.
The thymoma is clearly visible in the surgical field. The surgeon uses a bipolar grasper and a Maryland dissector to manipulate and dissect the surrounding tissue.

The surgeon utilizes a three-port approach from the right side of the chest, consisting of two 5mm ports and one 10mm port for the endoscope. The procedure begins with locating the inferior portion of the thymus, positioned just above the diaphragm.
The surgeon begins dissecting the thymoma from the back of the sternum, maintaining a distance from the thymus and thymoma to ensure a clear margin.
It's a really good idea to take as much of the thymic fat as you can.
The surgeon uses small, slim 5mm instruments, including a spatula or hook for monopolar diathermy, while preferring bipolar energy for its localized effect near the phrenic nerve.
And really good for doing a thymectomy.
The surgeon begins the dissection near the diaphragm, visible on the right side, and proceeds to follow the pericardium upwards, carefully removing thymic fat along with the specimen.
The surgeon identifies small branches extending from the reamer, with the right internal mammary artery visible at the top of the screen.
The surgeon begins the dissection approximately one centimeter medial to the right internal mammary artery, using small and slim instruments to carefully separate the thymic tissue.
Just a little note on the controls of the CMR system because you may not be familiar with this if you've only seen other systems.

You can see on the top left and the top right are the icons indicating the instrument base units. Obviously you don't have one, two, three, four all in a row because the base units can go anywhere around the patient.
So they're actually colour coordinated. So, the left instrument base unit is purple with the bipolar in there, and the right is the green base unit.

We've actually got a third cyan base unit ready for action, as you can see in the middle there, but Sven did not need it, as quite often you don't need a third retraction arm in the chest for a thymoma.
The surgeon removes the Maryland dissector, with the instrument's readiness for removal indicated by an icon in the top right of the screen.
And the really nice thing about the Versius is that you use just normal five mil ports.
So they're using allied medical five mil ports and they really easily can take the instruments out so you can put any other instruments in.
The bedside assistant uses a harmonic scalpel to apply energy to vessels as needed, swiftly maneuvering the instrument in and out of the surgical field.
The other good advantage is the instrument base units are really rather small, so there's plenty of space for an assistant, and it's very easy to take instruments out.
The surgeon dissects over the top of the pericardium, moving towards the pleura on the left-hand side, while maintaining a large margin. The dissection proceeds under the sternum.
The reamer is visibly pulsating as the surgeon navigates towards the left internal mammary artery, ensuring thorough exposure in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully advances up the chest, maintaining a safe distance from the thymoma, using the right internal mammary vein as a landmark, which becomes visible to the left of the Maryland dissector.
The surgeon carefully dissects the pleura, maintaining a large margin to ensure thorough removal of surrounding tissue.
The surgeon carefully dissects the pleura while following the right internal mammary vein down towards its insertion into the superior vena cava.
The surgeon follows a dissection path approximately half a centimeter medial to the phrenic nerve, maintaining careful proximity to this anatomical landmark.
The surgeon uses bipolar energy to safely dissect near the phrenic nerve, minimizing energy dissipation to protect surrounding structures.
The surgeon obtains a clear view of the superior vena cava (SVC) located below the phrenic nerve, with a small lymph node visible nearby, which is included in the surgical specimen.
The surgeon moves inferiorly to peel the thymus away from the pericardium, carefully separating the thymic tissue from the underlying cardiac structure.
The surgeon uses grasping forceps to lift the pericardium and diaphragm insertion forcefully, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon follows the phrenic nerve from below upwards, carefully dissecting along its path while maintaining a safe distance from the nerve.
The new IASLC classification does have a classification for lymph nodes, so it is important to take lymph nodes if you see them.
The surgeon identifies a lymph node within the surgical field, which is collected with the specimen for contribution to the TNM classification of thymomas.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the thymus from the pericardium, moving from above downwards. The pericardium is visibly clean, with no residual fat tissue.
The surgeon navigates the dissection towards the neck region, which is often considered a challenging part of the procedure.

The surgeon follows the innominate vein across the surgical field, identifying key landmarks such as the white internal mammary vein and the superior vena cava (SVC), while exercising caution around the innominate vein.
The surgeon identifies a small vessel potentially originating from the right internal mammary vein, examining its appearance and connection.

The video shows the blood supply to the thymus, highlighting the presence of inferior blood vessels, two lateral blood vessels from the internal mammary vein and artery, and additional supply from the thyrothymic ligament on both sides.

The surgeon identifies a vein coming from the reamer vein, preparing to address it using a harmonic device for vascular structures. A 5mm clip applicator is introduced, and the assistant places clips on either side of the possible reamer vein tributary into the thymus.
The surgeon uses the harmonic scalpel to safely divide a small vein in the middle of the surgical field, assisted by Sven.
The surgeon uses a green instrument unit with a Maryland dissector to carefully dissect the thymus away from the innominate vein within the chest cavity.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to expose the innominate vein, carefully maneuvering over it to improve visibility.
The surgeon prepares to identify the thymic vein, which often drains into the innominate vein, as the dissection progresses in the thymus region.

The surgeon identifies a vessel that requires clipping or division using the harmonic device. The surgical principle demonstrated involves dissecting along named structures such as the pericardium, innominate vein, or reamer vein, avoiding dissection through fatty tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects along named structures, avoiding fat, as the pericardium is visible with the aorta beneath it. Soft tissue is divided, ensuring no fat remains, and all tissue is removed with the thymoma.

You see the view really is excellent. moving the camera really nicely the Versius hand controls are extremely innovative there are no foot plates at all which allows you to stand up if you wish and you can control the camera with little joysticks on the top of your thumb and you control the opening and closing with your first finger now we're just looking for

The surgeon identifies potential vascular structures coming from the reamer vein or the innominate vein into the thymus. The right-hand arm is swapped out, removing the Maryland dissector and replacing it with a harmonic scalpel to safely divide the suspected vessel.

The surgeon addresses the left and right superior horns of the thymus, located just below the harmonic device, which extend over the innominate vein. The focus is on carefully extracting these structures from the neck area.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to dissect around the left and right superior horn of the thymus, visible above the instrument. The left-sided pleura is seen in the distance. The surgeon pulls down with the left hand to extract the thymus from the neck, aided by CO₂ insufflation creating space between the sternum and thymus.

The surgeon carefully divides the thymoma from the pericardium, ensuring complete separation of the soft tissue without leaving any fat behind. The thymoma appears relatively benign, and there is no need to dissect into the pericardium.

The surgeon tracks up into the right superior horn of the thymus, following its path into the neck for safe division. The innominate vein is visible in the distance, just below the Maryland dissector.

The surgeon uses a technique to pull the superior horn of the thymus down into the chest, allowing access to the thymic vein emerging from the innominate vein. The surgeon points to the innominate vein and the thymic vein below, preparing to safely divide the thymic vein.
The surgeon uses the Maryland dissector to manipulate and rotate tissue, with visible half-moon shaped structures appearing to the right of the instrument icons.
These show you how wound each instrument is and you can simply press the camera button joystick and it just unwinds all instruments very easily. A really nice feature actually.
The surgeon clips the thymic vein and uses a harmonic scalpel to divide it, demonstrating the ease of swapping between robotic and bedside instruments during the procedure.

one really nice thing about the Versys instruments is also it has a slightly different clutching mechanism so you can actually clutch the robot arms in different directions this is quite difficult to explain but actually once you get on it it has a real advantage because you can actually lock the arms in a different direction to the direction of your hand controls so you can keep your hand controls quite natural while they're in any rotational direction which is more comfortable for you some other systems you can get your hands a little bit contorted and there really is no choice about it whereas for Versys they have addressed this with a slightly different clutching pattern

The surgeon briskly pulls the superior horn out of the neck, exposing the thyro-thymic ligament. Using a harmonic device, the ligament is divided, revealing a small artery and vein within the structure.
The surgeon addresses the left superior horn, pulling it out from the neck while following the pleura. By staying medial to the pleura, the lemur artery and vein are kept safe during the procedure.

The assistant uses a harmonic device to pull down and divide tissue, ensuring clear visualization of the surgical field. The harmonic, which operates using ultrasound, is applied manually as it cannot be used with robotic instruments due to its design limitations.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to retract the thymus into the right chest, dividing the left side of the thymus from the left pleura. The clean pericardium is visible below the Maryland, and small bits of tissue are carefully separated, keeping the fat attached to the thymus. The left pleura is entered early in the procedure, with CO₂ insufflation in the chest.

The surgeon completes the final steps of removing the thymus from the neck, with the left and right superior horns already extracted. The thymus is now more mobile after being detached from the left pleura.

And with a few more pulls and a bit of dissection, we'll all be there. A really good job done by Sven and the team here, showing that really this Versius system is beautifully attuned for a thymectomy just using three ports, two five-mil ports, and an end-escape camera.

The surgeon uses bipolar diathermy to continue dissection along the left pleura, joining the previous dissection performed in the neck. Various instruments, including a spatula, hook, and energy-enabled scissors, are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully pulls the fat away from under the left phrenic nerve, ensuring the nerve remains safe while brushing away connective tissue. This technique effectively removes the fat from beneath the opposite phrenic nerve.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to carefully dissect around the superior vena cava (SVC) and the innominate vein, ensuring safety by staying above the innominate vein. The left superior horn of the thymus is being pulled out, with the final dissection steps involving the division of the thyrothymic ligament on the left side.
The surgeon carefully removes the final pieces of connective tissue to free the thymus, completing the dissection with precision.

So I hope you enjoyed that. I thought it was a really good job. It showed excellent working together as a team, really good action of the wristed instruments. I really encourage you to take a look at the hand controls on other videos. They really are great. And it's an open platform system with a three-D screen. And beautifully attuned for thinectomies. So congratulations again, Sven and the team. Look at that, beautiful.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the thymus, revealing the pericardium and underlying aorta, with the innominate vein and reamer vein visible and devoid of fatty tissue. The superior vena cava (SVC) and phrenic nerve are clearly dissected. The thymoma is exposed, measuring seven centimeters in size.
The surgeon increases the size of the right-sided port, inserts a specimen retrieval bag, and places the excised thymus tissue into the bag for removal.
